story:
I always wanted to escape; I just didn’t know how. Now the chance shows up, not earned, not even meant for me, but mine all the same. I can’t give it back, so I use it. At first I make a mess. Then the rules reveal themselves. My systems training helps, but in the end it’s hands-on: turn this shade, dim that one, learn what each tone can do.

I arrive late to work and walk through the stares. González, my boss, drags me into his office with his usual bark—no hello, no how are you. He starts the same speech as always, and I cut him off: today will be the worst day of your life. I watch his colors. Opaque grays, a brown straining toward scarlet, a buried blue that means more than he wants to admit. I nudge the palette. I dim the brown, strip the magenta of its gray veil, let the blue spread. Keep everything opaque; if the light can’t get in, sooner or later black rises like a tide. His face sinks. The job, the ladder, the family—all drain out of his eyes. Is something wrong? I ask, already knowing. He says no. I leave him there and try not to think about consequences, though it’s impossible not to. What I’m doing is dangerous. Maybe worse.

I’m almost out the door when another debt taps my shoulder. “Martina, can you come here a minute?” She walks toward me, and with each step I deepen the red and cool it with a purple edge so it won’t flare. Her pupils open; devotion paints her gaze. I say I have a surprise. In the bathroom I keep the red bright but cased in green so it doesn’t spin out. It is an art, I tell myself, choosing the right hue at the right time. The encounter happens fast. It’s clear how much she has been holding back. But I know this isn’t clean; bending feelings to my will isn’t the same as consent. When it’s over I tell her I’ll call later, to be patient. She nods, happy under the green’s calm shell.

On my way out I look at Quique, the doorman: a tired landscape of opaque purples with gray-green fog. My father always said, never fight with doormen; they have all the keys. I warm him with yellow and bright orange, back it with beige until loyalty settles in. He gives me his number, ready to help, and says that when I’m halfway down the block I’ll hear the crash, a van’s roof will cave in, and González will no longer be at his window. I tell myself I’m getting good at this. Too good.

I’m halfway down the block when I hear the crash, exactly as Quique said. A van’s roof caves in. Five floors up, González is no longer at his window. The crowd gathers; grays flare into alarm, burgundies stain into fear, greens turn gloomy, and there are always a few reds for morbid awe. I don’t stay. I didn’t push him, I tell myself, but I set the colors. That’s enough to make my stomach drop.

Everything on the street feels like a rainbow forced into my eyes. I buy dark glasses to dull the glare. In the mirror I can’t find my own colors; there’s only a blank where I should be. The hollow of that discovery opens, then closes. I remind myself to see the glass half full. I keep walking, laughing at how last Friday I was just a normal person with a boring job. Now I know how to move the palette. I feel the ache of what I can’t change in myself, but the rest of the world unfolds in shades I can bend. My possibilities, it seems, are endless.
